data.title: In Lawsuit Society, Elon Musk Still Posts Legally Wrong Nonsense — Because He’s Got Enough…
data.description:
Sometimes it’s very hard to know if Elon Musk is saying something completely idiotic and believing it or is simply lying. At the same time, people hang on every word he says and think his forecasts come from the mind of Einstein or something (in fact, one major investor has erroneously called him our modern day Einstein).

As just one example, he said that “money will stop being relevant at some point in the future” as a result of AI and robots doing all of the work. I’m sorry, but that is so ridiculously disconnected from reality and fanciful that it’s hard to think he could actually believe this, but I think he does. Yes, that is a dreamy utopian fantasy world that we’d love to see created, but that’s not how the world works and that’s never happening. There are many more examples in different fields of society, but the short and sweet of it is that Elon lives in a funny fantasy world in his head sometimes. Have hyperloops revolutionized transportation? Has he solved traffic in Los Angeles with tunnels? How are those low-cost solar shingles doing? By the way, SpaceX’s first mission to Mars was supposed to leave Earth in 2024.

“DOGE”

There are a handful of things from this week’s Tesla financials call that popped out to me in a similar way, as well as some other recent issues. I would just start with the DOGE issue, since it’s so dramatic and heartbreaking. On the call, Musk admitted that he “got a little too involved in politics” and that he “got carried away, frankly.” That sounds like a counterproductive hobby he picked up, but he dismantled some key institutions and norms in our society, and he abruptly crushed USAID, which effectively killed countless people.

“Public health models estimate that funding cuts and the dismantling of the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID) have resulted in roughly 600,000 to over 760,000 deaths, primarily among children and vulnerable populations in sub-Saharan Africa,” Google AI summarizes.

Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health’s Atul Gawande has estimated that shutting down USAID the way Musk did has resulted in hundreds of thousands of deaths. “Gawande cited an analysis in The Lancet that estimated that USAID assistance—aimed at combatting diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis, malaria, and polio, reducing maternal and child deaths, and fighting malnutrition—had saved 92 million lives over two decades.”

Admitting that he “got carried away,” but still not acknowledging the countless deaths he’s caused, doesn’t solve any problems created by his fantasy that 100% of USAID was fraud — a ridiculous, inane idea that it would take very little effort to debunk. Again, though, it’s just an example of Musk being very detached from reality — even while he wields so much power over it.

Anyway, human lives aside (sarcasm), DOGE at least saved the US money, right? NOPE. Independent analyses have estimated that DOGE result in a net loss to the US government of $27.5 billion to $165+ billion. Great work there, Elon. Way to save the United States from financial ruin. And the consequences keep rolling in….

Tesla FSD Claims

Getting back to Tesla, the matter of Elon Musk potentially breaking the law promoting Tesla FSD is getting into the news again. In December 2025, Musk tweeted that Full Self-Driving v14.2.1 would allow people to text while driving, “depending on [the] context of surrounding traffic.” Hmm … legally? I think we never covered this because we were waiting for more clarification about whether Tesla was taking on liability in the event of a crash if a “driver” was texting while driving. It seems that never happened. Musk also tweeted in May 2025 about how you could “Make your espresso on the road while your Tesla drives itself.” The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) has sent a request for information to Tesla, on July 2, to clarify these posts — since, really, the human behind the wheel using FSD is supposed to be paying attention at all times. The NHTSA also references Musk saying FSD can “operate in all conditions” and that a previous FSD update would “substantially reduce” the need for the human behind the wheel to pay attention to the car’s driving.

Does it matter if Elon Musk broke the law, or broke certain rules and regulations? He has done so before, with penalties being fines that would break most of us but which are essentially pennies to someone with the wealth Musk has. He also had to step down from certain leadership positions (CEO and Chairman) at Tesla in the past, but he was still running the show and was happy to just give himself the new title of Technoking. When you have the wealth and power of Elon Musk, it simply doesn’t matter. You get to play by your own rules.

US Tesla Sales

It’s been highlighted that Tesla climbed back above 50% of the US EV market in the second quarter, but that doesn’t mean its sales are up. In fact, its sales in the US were down 13% year over year (and 2025 wasn’t a good year for Tesla in the first place).

Musk implied that a lot more people were buying Tesla vehicles because of FSD. “And we’re seeing in locations that have FSD approved, we’re seeing a very high take rate of FSD,” he said. “And in fact, I think, for a lot of people, they’re actually buying Tesla full self-driving with a car attached as opposed to a car with FSD. They’re coming into our stores in the US and they want the full self-driving and with whatever car comes with it essentially. So, clearly, this is a significant demand driver. And as we get approval for FSD in different countries, I think we’ll see a similar uptick in demand. So, yes, it’s looking very good on that front.”

But Tesla sales were down in the US … the main market by far where people can use FSD. So, that’s a little confusing.

Meanwhile, USA Today shares that “Tesla has reported more than 200 crashes involving its automated driver assistance systems, such as Autopilot and Full Self-Driving, in recent months. This data was compiled by the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.”

Tesla just launched very limited Robotaxi deployment in Tampa a few days ago, and one of the vehicles was already spotted driving the wrong way on a one-way street. “A driver spotted a Tesla robotaxi going the wrong way down a one-way street a day after the company rolled out its driverless taxi service in Tampa.” 😬

And Then There Are The Robots …

Tesla’s car business can dwindle, though, according to Musk, because its humanoid robot business will be MUCH BIGGER. That’s the pitch. Tesla is an AI and robot company now. And these robots are supposed to eliminate poverty, be able to perform surgery, bring in an “age of amazing abundance,” and just basically solve all of the world’s problems.

Well, for the time being, after hyping plans to produce and sell 1 million of these robots a year initially from the company’s Fremont factory, and planning to produce and sell 10 million a year from its Texas factory, Musk is now cautioning people that it’s actually really hard to scale up production of these robots (who knew?), not to mention make them do all the things we’d want.

“I really want to emphasize here that the production scaling challenge is very substantial,” said Musk. “This is going to be the hardest product to scale manufacturing that we’ve ever made at Tesla because everything on the robot is new. And the difficulty of scaling the production ramp is proportionate to the newness of the parts.”

Hmm, remember “Production Hell” for the Tesla Model 3 and how that almost bankrupted the company? Remember how it took years to get the Model X into production because of the falcon-wing doors and giant windshield? Apparently, this is going to be much harder than any of that. Talk about pumping the brakes….

Musk also essentially claimed that other robot companies were cheating and lying, and that Tesla really was the sole leader in the race to build useful humanoid robots, no matter how it may look.

“So you’ve probably seen lots of impressive demonstrations of robots on the internet,” said Musk. “But those demonstrations you’re seeing are preprogrammed or remote controlled. So there is no humanoid robot that is actually able to do generalized tasks. Optimus will be the first one that is capable of doing that.”

Never mind that Tesla has deceivingly deployed teleoperated robots for hype demonstrations, and Figure’s CEO has denied doing such a thing despite people assuming it has done the same.

“At one point on Wednesday’s earnings call, Musk explained that he was ill, as though it was an excuse for something, though it’s not clear why he would say that. He sounded perfectly fine, even if he was clearly stressing about Optimus,” Gizmodo writes.

Hmm, the hype machine may be falling apart on the robots now….

It’s no wonder Tesla stock collapsed following the financials call.

Checking the stock price just now, I see that it is down 29% from the start of the year. It’s down almost 20% in the past five days.

What does the future bring? Who knows? But I can guarantee it’s not utopia, no need to work, and an “age of amazing abundance” thanks to Tesla AI and robots.

data.title: Germany Is Subsidizing Hydrogen Truck Purchases, Not Battery-Electric Ones
data.description:
Germany’s latest hydrogen truck programme attracted 526 applications seeking €455 million from a €220 million funding pool. That has been presented as evidence that hydrogen freight is finally finding a market. A more revealing fact sits inside the programme design: Germany is offering purchase support for hydrogen trucks while battery-electric trucks appear to have no comparable current federal vehicle-purchase subsidy.

The hydrogen programme can cover up to 80% of a vehicle’s additional eligible cost relative to diesel and as much as 50% of eligible station costs. This is not a minor technology-neutral incentive available equally to competing zero-emission drivetrains. It directly reduces the sticker-price disadvantage of hydrogen trucks while helping build the specialized stations they require. Germany’s visible federal support for battery-electric freight is now concentrated more heavily on charging infrastructure, motorway corridors and other operating-system components.

That difference can distort the fleet purchase decision. A current European N3 diesel tractor costs about €150,000, battery electric about €300,000 and hydrogen fuel cell about €425,000. If Germany covers up to 80% of the hydrogen truck’s €275,000 premium over the diesel truck, the public contribution could approach €220,000, reducing the operator’s effective purchase cost to roughly €205,000. A procurement manager could therefore see the subsidized hydrogen truck as cheaper to buy than an unsubsidized battery-electric truck, even though the grant has not changed the drivetrain’s energy requirements or fuel costs. For contrast, equivalent battery electric trucks in China can be purchased for €80,000, really testing the question about exactly why European OEMs are charging so much.

This is where the distinction between purchase economics and freight economics matters. A vehicle subsidy is received once. The operator pays for energy, maintenance, station access, uptime and financing across every kilometer the truck travels. Hydrogen still requires energy to be converted, compressed, distributed, stored and dispensed before a fuel cell converts it back into electricity. Battery trucks use electricity through a shorter chain, and that efficiency advantage reappears in the operating account after the purchase subsidy has done its work.

Battery-electric trucks are not free of policy or infrastructure challenges. Their purchase prices remain above diesel, depot grid connections can be slow, motorway charging is incomplete and financing and residual-value markets are still developing. Those conditions could support a temporary, declining purchase bridge for high-mileage electric trucks while production volumes and used-vehicle markets mature. Instead, Germany has chosen to buy down a large share of the hydrogen premium while asking battery-electric trucks to overcome their own first-cost barrier.

That is difficult to reconcile with the direction identified by the French and German economic councils, which put stationary-charged battery-electric trucks at the centre of near-term freight decarbonization. Battery-electric trucks offer the shorter energy chain, lower energy costs and the clearer fit with the power system. Hydrogen can still be tested for unusual duty cycles, but the more energy-intensive pathway should have to demonstrate that direct charging, corridor charging, swapping or operational adjustment cannot meet the route.

The programme’s asymmetry is more consequential because freight electrification is becoming a system problem rather than a vehicle problem. China is linking electric trucks with ports, mines, logistics parks, depots, charging corridors, battery swapping, grid planning, standards, maintenance and fleet operations. Europe is building many of the same components more incrementally. CATL and Octopus Energy’s Swaptopus venture also suggests that the commercial competition may extend into battery ownership, leasing, state-of-health information and route-energy management.

Against that transition, Germany is using public money to preserve a parallel hydrogen fuel system while leaving the primary electric drivetrain without an equivalent purchase incentive. Hydrogen trucks receive help with the vehicle premium and their stations. Battery-electric trucks receive support for charging infrastructure and then rely on their lower operating costs to make the vehicle purchase work.

That does not amount to a fair comparison among zero-emission pathways. It risks making the drivetrain with weaker lifetime economics look more attractive at procurement because the state absorbs more of its disadvantage. An oversubscribed funding call then measures the resulting demand for subsidized equipment and is interpreted as market validation.

The real evidence will arrive after deployment. Fleets will have to report utilization, fuel costs, station uptime, maintenance, residual value and whether they buy another hydrogen truck after operating the first subsidized tranche. Until then, Germany has shown that generous hydrogen purchase support can generate applications. It has not shown that hydrogen trucks can outperform battery-electric trucks as commercial freight assets.

The full TFIE Strategy Briefing analysis quantifies how the purchase signal can be reversed by the subsidy, how the comparison changes again when energy is measured per 100 km, and how low station throughput can turn infrastructure grants into continuing support for the pump price. It also tests Germany’s corridor programme against China’s electric-freight buildout and the emerging CATL–Octopus platform model.

Read Germany Is Still Trying To Make Hydrogen Trucks Happen and subscribe to TFIE Strategy Briefing for the capex comparison, operating-cost reversal, station-utilization test and freight-platform scorecard.

data.title: Hyundai Motor Group Launches AllDayEnergy, Its Global V2X Service
data.description:
• New global name brings Hyundai Motor Group’s V2X services together under a single identity
  • AllDayEnergy integrates Smart Charging (V1G), Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) and Vehicle-to-Home (V2H) services under a single global identity
  • Starting with V1G smart charging, rollout will begin in the UK first via the Kia App in H2 2026, with planned expansion across Europe, the U.S. and Korea
  • Services will be available through the vehicle apps of Hyundai Motor and Kia

Hyundai Motor Group (The Group) today announced the launch of AllDayEnergy, its new global Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) energy services.

AllDayEnergy brings the Group’s existing V2X services together under a single global identity, providing customers with a consistent and trusted experience across different markets and service types.

The service will initially cover V1G smart charging, before Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) and Vehicle-to-Home (V2H). The Group will begin rolling out AllDayEnergy in the UK via the Kia App in the second quarter of 2026, with planned expansion to Europe, the U.S., and Korea.

“When we launch AllDayEnergy through our Kia App later this year, we will be taking a significant step towards establishing our identity as a smart energy service provider. We will continue to expand our AllDayEnergy service globally, allowing customers to seamlessly optimize their energy usage, reduce their energy costs, and contribute to grid sustainability.” —Woong Tae Hwang, Vice President and Head of the EV Energy Strategy Group at Hyundai Motor Group

What is AllDayEnergy?

AllDayEnergy is the Group’s unified global services that connect electric vehicle (EV) batteries with homes and electricity grids.

V2X technology enables bidirectional energy flow between an electric vehicle and the grid. This means an EV can act as a mobile energy storage system, rather than simply consuming electricity through charging.

Customers can charge their vehicles when electricity costs are lower, then use the stored energy later or supply it to an external system when needed. Depending on the market and service type, this can help customers manage charging costs, support household energy needs, and participate in energy programs offered by electric utilities.

By bringing these services under the AllDayEnergy name, the Group will establish a common experience across its global V2X portfolio.

Which Services Will AllDayEnergy Represent?

AllDayEnergy will bring together several types of energy services:

  • V1G: Adjusts the timing of vehicle charging in response to real-time electricity prices, grid conditions and customer preferences.
  • V2G: Enables an EV battery to return energy to the grid when required.
  • V2H: Allows energy stored in an EV battery to support household electricity needs, including backup power during power outages.

The Group has already developed and operated V2X services in several regions.

In Korea, the Group is piloting V2G technology in Jeju Special Self-Governing Province. In the U.S., it has deployed V2H services that provide emergency household backup power during events such as major wildfires. In the Netherlands, it has introduced V2G and smart charging services that optimize vehicle charging in response to dynamic electricity prices.

How can customers access AllDayEnergy?

AllDayEnergy services will be integrated into the vehicle apps of Hyundai Motor and Kia. Initial rollout will begin in UK Kia App, when the service is added later this year. The Group will expand the service to additional markets.

The name AllDayEnergy reflects the Group’s vision of making smart energy management an integral part of customers’ daily lives and advancing its transition into a smart energy solutions provider.

data.title: EVs Take 34.5% Share In France — Renault & Tesla Dominate
data.description:
The second quarter of 2026 saw plugin EVs take 34.5% share in France, up from 23.9% in Q2 2025. BEV share grew very strongly YoY, while PHEV share was slightly down. Overall Q2 auto volume was 457,688 units, up some 6% YoY. The Tesla Model Y was the best-selling BEV in Q2, just ahead of the Renault 5.

2026 Q2 auto sales saw combined plugin EVs take 34.5% share in France, with full electrics (BEVs) at 28.3% and plugin hybrids (PHEVs) at 6.2%. These figures compare YoY against 23.9% combined, 17.1% BEVs and 6.8% PHEVs.

In volume terms, BEVs were up a massive 75% YoY to 120,479 units, whilst PHEVs were down around 4%, at 28,281 units. This significant BEV boost is partly due to improvements in incentives in 2026, and partly due to soaring fuel prices.

Both petrol-only and diesel-only continue a steep decline (now summing to 17.9% share) with even default powertrains now being HEV or MHEV (some degree of electric assist). As emissions rules tighten (and oil continues to be expensive), these will be replaced by plugins, and eventually almost entirely BEVs.

Q2 saw a slight uptick in petrol-only sales, but this is likely just a temporary “selling-off old stock” and they will be back to decline in the coming quarters.

Best-Selling BEV Models

The best-selling BEV in Q2 2026 was again the Tesla model Y (11,695 units), with – once-again – the Renault 5 very close behind (10,579 units). Indeed, all of the top 3 spots saw a similar pattern to last quarter, though with the lead 2 models pulling a bit further ahead from the Renault Scenic in third (now dipping to 6,233 units).

Renault did very well to take 4 out of the top 6 spots, with the new Renault Twingo (in 6th) already ramping to strong volumes since its March debut.

The Twingo is very close to the Dacia Spring on specs, with just a 10% larger battery (and range), for about 5% higher official MSRP. Even noting substantial real world discounting on the Spring, the Twingo is additionally eligible for the  “Coup de Pouce” bonus incentive (ballpark of €3.5k to €5.7k, depending on household income), whereas the Spring is ineligible.

As a result, the Twingo ends up being around €2000 less expensive than the Spring, all said and done. This largely explains why the Renault has close to double the sales of the Dacia. Either way, it’s good to see more examples of affordable (if rather short range) BEVs coming available.

As usual, there is very limited publicly available data on model sales, so we can’t detect the initial foray of new debutants onto the French market. Take a look at nearby European markets to get a sense of what new models are becoming available.

Outlook

With the revised 2026 incentive structure as a carrot, plus the real-world stick of high and volatile oil prices, BEVs have seen a huge step up in Q2 2026, and this trend will likely continue in some form throughout the rest of the year.

The French macroeconomy remains cool. From YoY GDP growth of 1.1% in 2025 Q4, there was a slowdown to 0.9% in 2026 Q1, and a forecast further drop to 0.6% for Q2 (official figures not year published). Headline inflation has hovered either side of 2% in recent months. Interest rates, which had been flat for 12 months at 2.25%, stepped up to 2.5% since early June. Manufacturing PMI has hovered around 50 points in 2026.

What do you expect the remainder of 2026 to reveal about the EV transition in France? Please share your comments and thoughts in the discussion below.

data.title: EVs At 41.4% Share In The UK — Tesla Leading BEV Brand
data.description:
2026 Q2 saw plugin EVs at 41.4% share in the UK, up from 34.2% in Q2 2025. BEV share grew decently year on year, whilst PHEV share grew modestly. Overall Q2 auto volume was 523,075 units, up some 13% YoY. Tesla was the best-selling BEV brand in Q2.

2026 Q2 auto sales saw combined plugin EVs at 41.4% share in the UK, with full electrics (BEVs) at 28.1% and plugin hybrids (PHEVs) at 13.3%. These figures compare YoY against 34.2% combined, 22.7% BEVs and 11.6% PHEVs.

In volume terms, Q2’s BEV sales were up 40% YoY, to 146,965 units. PHEVs were up by 30% to 69,466 units.

Cumulatively, by the end of Q2, BEV share stood at 25.0% (from 21.6% YoY). PHEVs share stood at 13.0% (from 10.3% YoY). The UK’s legacy industry lobby, the SMMT, is still whining that the “33% ZEV” target for 2026 is going to be “too hard” to meet, and needs watering down. But the reality is that the progress this year in relation to the (now higher) target is only a tiny fraction behind where it was at this point last year (in relation to last year’s lower “28% ZEV” target).

In addition, EV momentum has picked up significantly across Q2, from a slow-start Q1, no doubt encouraged by rising fuel prices due to the war of aggression against Iran. In concrete terms, BEVs only grew YoY volume 14.5% in Q1, but accelerated to a substantial 40.4% volume growth in Q2. You can see this in the angle of the Q1 to Q2 uptick in the graph below, much steeper than uptick in the same period last year, or any other recent year.

Ignoring and obscuring this positive recent Q2 trajectory, the SMMT are trying to push a “it’s too hard” narrative. Why? Because they want to water down the pace of EV progress regardless, and milk more profit from their sunk investments in ICE technology. Policy makers should inform themselves of the real trend data, and ignore the crocodile tears of the combustion lobby.

Combined combustion-only share in Q2 fell to a record low of 45.2%, and should end Q4 close to 40%.

Best-Selling BEV Brands

The best-selling Q2 BEV brand in the UK was once again Tesla, with market share improving YoY from 9.8% (Q2 2025) to 11.0%. In second place was Volkswagen at 7.2% (from 9.5% YoY), and in third was Kia, at 6.7% share (from 4.7% YoY).

As well as Tesla, other brands seeing good YoY growth in market share were MG, Kia, Mini, Leapmotor, Jaecoo and Toyota. Amongst those losing notable YoY share were Volkswagen, BMW and Peugeot.

We don’t have access to detailed enough sales data to discern all the new BEV models which debuted in Q2, but keep an eye on our reports on nearby markets to get a rough idea of what new models are likely arriving. A few models which were visible in the UK data were the Kia EV2, the Aion V, and the new Porsche Cayenne.

Outlook

Unless policy makers allow themselves to be duped by legacy auto, the UK’s ZEV mandate, plus the continuing modest purchase incentives for BEVs, mean BEVs will continue on a decent growth trajectory in 2026.

The overall UK economy remains very weak, with Q1 2026 (latest) figures recently revised down to just 0.9% YoY GDP growth. Headline inflation reduced to 2.6% at the end of Q2, from 3.3% as of the end of Q1. This seems strange given that transport fuel prices have increased by over 20% over the period. Interest rates have been flat at 3.75% since mid December. Manufacturing PMI stood at around 52 points at the end of Q2, up from 51 points at end of Q1.

What are your expectations for the UK auto market for the remainder of this year? Please share your thoughts and perspectives in the discussion below.

data.title: Tesla Stocks Down, Tesla EV Market Share Up: What Comes Next?
data.description:
What goes down could go up, so Thursday’s dramatic dip in the price of Tesla stocks could be a temporary blip. For that matter, the company continues to hold the frontrunner position in EV sales within the challenging market of the US by an epically wide margin, so what’s not to like? Still, the sudden nosedive is calling renewed attention to shortcomings in the Robotaxi space.

Questions Linger Over Robotaxi

Tesla stock has persisted in and around the high 300’s for a while now, off its December 2025 peak of $498 but substantially higher than where it stood in 2010, when the company’s initial iteration as Tesla Motors launched an IPO at $17. That’s not a typo. The IPO was $17.

Tesla stock is still sky high, but Thursday’s nosedive has sparked some alarm. At the closing bell on Thursday, Tesla was down to its lowest since last August, trading at $320.

The downward drift followed a Q2 earnings report from CEO Elon Musk, which featured notes on the number of unsold vehicles year-to-date (22,000 so far) and crashes related to the company’s driver assistance technology (more than 200, for those of you keeping score at home).

That was hardly reassuring news, and Musk did not lighten the mood when he dove into a progress report on the company’s Robotaxi driverless ride hailing service (see CleanTechnica’s complete Robotaxi coverage here).

“Our goals are very ambitious for Robotaxi, but we do need to be cautious about causing any accidents or causing any harm to anyone,” Musk said in widely cited remarks.

“We’ve opened up a number of cities in Florida and in Texas and obviously in the Bay Area. So we’ll continue to scale, I think, very, very rapidly on – with it looks like more than 10% a week in terms of miles driven. So it’s a very high compound growth rate,” he elaborated.

Promises, Promises

As for why the assurance of a high compound growth rate did not reassure investors, that’s no mystery. Musk has been over-promising on Tesla’s Robotaxi business from the beginning. In January of this year, CleanTechnica editor Zachary Shahan outlined the gap between vision and reality:

“Elon Musk said last July that the company’s new Robotaxi business would cover about 50% of the US population by the end of 2025, and then when it was obvious that wasn’t happening, there was big hype around a commitment that they’d be rolling out completely driverless cars (no human supervisors) before the end of the year. It’s now more than 3 months beyond the end of 2025 and there’s no apparent progress on this.”

“There might be only one driverless Tesla in Austin, but certainly seems to be fewer than 10. That is not covering at least 50% of the US population unless you expect people to travel to Austin from around the country to try out the revolutionary Tesla Robotaxi,” Shahan emphasized.

Tesla did extend the launch of the Robotaxi into Florida earlier this month, initially in Miami with Tampa and Orlando following. However, it didn’t take long for things to go south.

“A driver spotted a Tesla robotaxi going the wrong way down a one-way street a day after the company rolled out its driverless taxi service in Tampa,” News Channel 8 TV in Florida reported on July 22.

“The dashcam video, submitted by Jon Francisco, appears to show a Tesla robotaxi going north on North Armenia Avenue at the intersection of West Cypress Street in West Tampa,” continued Channel 8 reporter Sarah Rains.

Tesla Continues To Dominate The US EV Market, For Now

CleanTechnica readers were also quick to note shortcomings in the Florida launch, particularly in regards to Orlando. If you have any insights to contribute, drop a note in the comment thread.

Robotaxi troubles or not, Tesla remains the single largest seller of EVs in the US, capturing just over 50% of the US market in Q2. That’s quite a feat considering all the reputational baggage Musk has heaped upon the company in his capacity as head of Trump’s DOGE budget-cutting office, among other extracurricular activities, though it is significantly down from the 80% share Tesla claimed in 2019 at its peak.

After 2019 other automakers began to crowd into Tesla’s market share, and still the company continues to maintain its leadership position. As for the coming months, it wouldn’t be much of a surprise to see Tesla continue clawing back a bit more of its market share over the next few quarters, as other EV makers curtail their 100% EV plans in favor of plug-in hybrids.

That may or may not persist beyond 2027. While General Motors and Ford did pull back on their EV plans last year after the $7,500 federal EV tax credit died a premature death, both automakers have persistently affirmed their intent to focus on EV affordability in the coming years, with Ford on track to produce a to-be-named midsized electric pickup truck and GM — well, who knows? As of this writing, GM is still pitching the 2027 Bolt as “the most affordable EV in America,” though rumor has it that GM plans to retire the Bolt, again.

Tesla is also going to face competition from other all-electric startups. While Lucid has failed to gain traction, Rivian has already established a solid footprint and it has somehow managed to secure billions in Energy Department loans to build another factory in Georgia. Another all-electric startup, the Jeff Bezos-backed Slate Auto, expects to launch its first deliveries later this year.

Another startup to watch is TELO Trucks, which is marketing a city-scaled electric pickup truck inspired by the pint-sized “Kei” cars of Japan. The automaker was rather quiet earlier this year, but on July 14 TELO announced that its forthcoming MT1 EV — which is about the size of a Mini Cooper — successfully towed a 5,800-pound Pebble Flow full-size travel trailer with an assist from Pebble’s “Easy Tow” system.

Then there’s Dacora. The New York startup is pitching a hand-crafted, retro-styled luxury EV featuring a wooden hood (you get to pick which wood). The dashboard, also made of wood, does this weird thing where it — well, lets let Dacora tell it:

“This is not a touchscreen. It’s a more personal, intuitive relationship with your car — activated by subtle movement, guided by muscle memory, and tailored to the way you think, not the way machines do. In your Dacora, driving becomes more than routine — it becomes an awakened experience,” the company states somewhat loftily.

Interested? Reserve yours here for a refundable deposit of $50,000.

Photo: The Tesla Robotaxi service is launching in more US cities, but investors were not amused after CEO Elon Musk presented the company’s Q2 report (screenshot, courtesy of Tesla via CleanTechnica archive).

data.title: EV Charging Infrastructure Partnerships Starting to Grow In Kenya
data.description:
Kenya is, surprisingly, one of the countries that is lagging behind when it comes to the adoption of electric cars. One sector that has seen some significant action in Kenya, however, is the electric bus segment, catalysed by innovative pay-as-you-drive programs which are essentially energy as a service channels. For example, BasiGo’s pay-as-you-drive offering unbundles ownership of the battery from the vehicle. Transport operators then get access to a bus at a price that is more comparative to a diesel bus and then pay a subscription for an energy bundle packaged as a battery rental that includes all charging, all scheduled maintenance, and roadside assistance. Through the pay-as-you-drive service, bus owners are also provided with real-time monitoring and comprehensive analytics of every electric bus in their fleet.

As this charging infrastructure grows to support an expansion bus fleet, there will be room to open it up to the public to charge their electric cars. The main driver of a successful charging business is utilisation. With Kenya’s low penetration of electric cars, a stand-alone infrastructure business becomes a challenge. But a charging network underpinned by the captive electric bus fleet becomes promising. Therefore, the increasing adoption of electric buses in Kenya could play a key role in supporting the adoption of electric cars, as EV owners’ concerns around the availability of adequate infrastructure, and ultimately range anxiety, will slowly but surely be alleviated.

BasiGo, a provider of electric buses and an energy solutions provider, has recently partnered with Rubis Energy Kenya to expand fast-charging infrastructure along Kenya’s key transport corridors, supporting the growth of electric mobility across the country. The first charging station under the partnership is now operational at the Rubis fuel service station in Sabaki, Athi River, with additional sites in Meru, Nanyuki, and Nyeri scheduled to be commissioned later on this month. BasiGo says the stations will be open to the public and will serve electric buses and vans, trucks, passenger vehicles, and other compatible electric vehicles, expanding access to reliable charging infrastructure and making electric vehicle operations practical across intercity routes in Kenya.

The Sabaki charging station is equipped with both CCS2 and GB/T DC 100kW fast chargers, capable of charging a typical passenger vehicle in under one hour, with compatibility extending across a wide range of commercial electric vehicles. All sites under the partnership will feature similar charging technology and offer charging at an indicative rate of KES 48 per kWh, ensuring consistent and reliable experience across the network. It looks like this will be the formula going forward. Traditionally, vehicles had been coming mostly from Europe, hence the adoption of the CCS2 standard. However, an increasing number of vehicles are now starting to come from China, hence GB/T is now also gaining traction. 

Moses Nderitu, Managing Director, Kenya, BasiGo, said: “The question is no longer whether operators and the public want to go electric, but whether the infrastructure is in place to support them at scale. Every charging station we deploy along key transport corridors strengthens the business case for electric mobility and makes intercity electric transport more practical, reliable, and commercially viable. Partnerships like this are critical to building the confidence needed to accelerate EV adoption across the country.”

Frederic Maupetit, Managing Director, Rubis Energy Kenya, said: “This partnership between BasiGo and Rubis Energy Kenya is a pivotal step in powering Kenya’s sustainable future. By putting our retail network behind the next generation of mobility, we are making EV travel practical for Kenyans along major transport corridors. This is a clear signal of Rubis’ commitment to leading the energy transition here.”

The partnership between Rubis and BasiGo is an interesting one. Rubis has network coverage of more than 300 fuel stations in Kenya and more in the East and Southern African region, and BasiGo has one of the largest networks of DC fast chargers for electric vehicles in East Africa. This partnership shows that traditional players are starting to collaborate with EV charging infrastructure providers, unlocking synergies that could accelerate the rollout of electric vehicle infrastructure to support the adoption of electric vehicles in Kenya and in the region. It will continue to expand the EV infrastructure needed to support reliable, scalable electric mobility, with additional charging sites planned across key transport corridors in Kenya and the wider region.

Images courtesy of BasiGo

data.title: XPENG L03 Impresses In The Details: Part 2
data.description:
This is the second part of a detailed look at the design the new XPENG L03. Before reading this, I recommend reading Part 1.

There has been a lot of discussion about rear cargo space. By the standard of liquid volume up to the top of the rear seat back, the L03 has 539l of cargo space. By cubic measurement up to the top of the rear seatback, which is a German standard, it has 367l of cargo volume. Tesla measures their cargo area in liquid volume up to the roof, which is not a standard measurement, so that makes it difficult to compare based on spec sheets. I can confidently say that the L03 has more room behind the rear seats than a Model YL has behind its third row and has less than the standard Model Y has behind its second row, although the exact amount is hard to say without an apples-to-apples measurement. At 0.9 m (35.4”) from the rear hatch to the second-row seatbacks, the L03’s rear cargo space is long enough to carry full-sized suitcases lengthwise. That is likely more than enough for many drivers. Rather than fixating on the different volume measurements online, I recommend people go to a showroom and take a look in real life to see if the amount of cargo space is enough for their needs.

However, if you really do need a little more room, XPENG has a larger L05 on the way, which falls between the Model Y and YL in length. This car has already appeared in Chinese MIIT regulatory filings, and I saw a camouflaged prototype parked outside of XPENG’s Munich offices.

Many XPENG models have previously done without a frunk, but the BEV L03 has a decently sized compartment at 102l, big enough for a carryon suitcase. It also has a drain plug, making it easy to clean or to potentially be used as a cooler. Also under the hood is a cap to fill wiper fluid and access to the accessory battery under a small cover. The REEV loses the frunk to place a range-extending engine under the cover. By opening a small flap, people can check the oil level through a traditional dipstick that has been unfortunately disappearing from many ICE models.

In total, there are 37 different storage locations. Notably, a 10l drawer under the rear seat, large enough for things like shoes, snacks or bedding. That bedding could come in handy. By removing the front headrests and reclining fully, the front seat backs meet the rear seat bottoms, forming a bed, which could be useful to take a nap during charging or for camping.

The car is offered with 58.3 and 71.2 kWh LFP batteries on a 400V platform. 3C charging takes the battery from 10-80% in 20 minutes. That makes it charge a bit slower than XPENG’s 800V cars, but still faster than a Tesla. It also supports 6kW V2L.

The Standard Range model has 445 km of range and 0-100 km/h in 7.5s, while a Long Range version improves that to 520 km of range and 0-100 km/h in 6.6s. Both of those models have the same 180 kWh motor, but XPENG tuned the base version to be a bit more relaxed. In Europe, an AWD Performance version is also offered with 440 km of range and 285 kW of power, good for 0-100 km/h in 4.5s.

XPENG is also offering its EREV version, with up to 215 km of WLTP battery range. Beyond the battery range, total range extends over 1000 km with a 1.5l gasoline motor up front that is not connected to the motor in the rear. This range is far more than what many European drivers would expect from this type of vehicle and more than PHEV competitors.

In Europe, smaller vehicles are more likely to be used for towing than in the US.  A detachable tow bar is offered across all models, rated for up to 1500 kg. For those looking to tow long distances on holidays but have a useable EV range during the week, the EREV could be an attractive option.

While all versions have intelligent driving capability, the Ultra version also adds 3 Turing chips for full VLA 2.0 full scenario navigation. We’ll discuss VLA 2.0 more in a future article.

At a pre-tax price of 265,933 NOK ($27,600 USD) in Norway, the L03 is less expensive than the least expensive EV in the US, the Chevy Bolt. Compared to that car, the L03 is dramatically higher quality. It is also a significantly larger vehicle, at over a foot longer.

However, that brings up a potential criticism. While it hits the sweet spot of the automotive market, the L03 is also not much smaller than XPENG’s G6. It also feels premium, and there are clearly areas where costs could be cut. Considering how tight some European roads can be, particularly in the south, there is clearly room for a smaller car.  Maybe we could see a “L01” subcompact?

On the other hand, several of us wondered if XPENG could bring some of the Chinese market options to global markets, like refrigerators and zero gravity seats with 14 points of massage. As Chinese cars become established, could we see domestic market versions gain popularity with fans in other markets? Kind of like JDM-focused models or Wolfsburg-edition Volkswagen models in the US?

Overall, while the L03 looks good on screen, it looks better in person. And it seems better as you explore the details, feel the materials and notice the build quality. Many of us at the event were impressed by details that we discovered without XPENG bringing them up.

Of course, like even the best relationships, I am sure some frustrations might arise over time. However, from what I have seen exploring the details of the L03, this XPENG could turn many potential customers into loyal fans.

data.title: XPENG L03 Impresses In The Details: Part 1
data.description:
While we have written about the XPENG L03 before, looking at a vehicle on screens and spec sheets only tells you so much. What really sets vehicles apart is the execution. The fit and finish, material feel, and design details.

The following video shows my first impressions through the fog of jetlag, having just landed in Munich a few hours earlier.

The focus customers for the L03 are couples in their 30’s to mid-40’s who either have young families or are looking to start a family. They are trying to balance the need for practicality with a desire for technology in a car that isn’t boring. This compares to the somewhat older typical Tesla buyer, particularly within China. You can see the difference in customer focus reflected in many of the design choices. While I may be a bit older at 50, the design choices also reflect many of my preferences.

For the European market, the color choice at launch focuses on black, white, and silver/gray, with just one shade of purple. While color has caught on in China, the European market seems a bit more restrained. In discussions with different people at the launch, most of us preferred the purple color that shifts shade in different light. That also went for many of us who tend to gravitate to other colors. I personally think it would look great in a blue or green. It will be interesting to see if the Chinese market leads to the color’s return to popularity globally and if more colors become available in the future.

The L03 is positioned below the G6 against vehicles like the ID.5 in the European market. However, it is a little larger, more powerful, and better equipped at that price point. In many ways, it is more comparable to a Model Y. From mirror to mirror, they are the exact same width of 1920 mm, although the L03 has a slightly wider body, due to frameless mirrors. A track width of 1,640 mm front and 1,658 mm rear is also a little wider than a Model Y.

The length is 4650 mm, a couple of inches longer than a RAV4, but several inches shorter than a Model Y. Overlaying profile images of the two vehicles at scale, the difference comes mostly from the front overhang and rear bumper trim. It is also a few fractions of an inch lower than the Y, but the Lo3 has a flatter roof over the passenger compartment before sloping down at a steeper angle over the rear cargo area. In person, it doesn’t look noticeably different in size. However, with the wheels pushed farther out toward the corners, combined with curves and design details, it makes the L03 look a bit more muscular.

The L03 has floating calipers front and rear, behind 18” wheels in the base model. The Ultra and AWD Performance models have 20” wheels with yellow caliper covers that look like fixed calipers, but the actual calipers are still floating. The lower profile tires on the larger wheels also likely contribute to a sportier feel. Behind the wheels are MacPherson struts up front and a multilink suspension at the rear. Unlike the Chinese market, European models do not have adaptive dampening. However, the Chinese tuning was more focused on comfort, while the European version is intentionally a little stiffer and sportier.

Having driven the next P7, XPENG knows how to do a well-balanced, sporty suspension. While I was not yet able to drive the L03 yet, I was able to ride along in a prototype Ultra AWD with 20” wheels, mostly using VLA 2.0. Clearly, somebody had been testing the cornering earlier, based on the fresh wear pattern on the tires. While I couldn’t coax the driver to push it while we were onboard, the suspension seemed to have a good amount of feel. You got a decent sense of what the tires were doing without it feeling harsh going over bumps. It was quiet, without rattles, and the chassis felt stiff. I didn’t notice body roll. The driver also said it had good steering feel. Overall, the ride felt like it could offer a good balance, although it would take some time behind the wheel with some spirited driving to know for sure.

Despite its length, the L03 passenger compartment is longer and wider than the Model Y. Due to its low floor and a roof line that stays relatively flat until tapering off behind the passengers, headroom is more than adequate. I had the pleasure to ride with longtime CleanTechnica friend Roger Atkins, who at 6’2” easily fit front and rear without taking off his signature cowboy hat. This is helped by subtle indentations in the rear headliner, which was a recommendation that I gave XPENG on the P7+. I can’t say for sure that this is in response to my feedback, but I’ll act like it was my idea.

The low floor and large door openings make it easy to get in and out. For me, getting in was like sitting in a chair. I didn’t have to step up or squat down. This should make it useful for people with mobility problems. Due to the roofline, the rear seats also sit up a bit higher than in some EVs. In front, the seats and steering wheel had a good range of motion, making it easy for people of different sizes to find a comfortable seating position.

The interior of the L03 is fully vegan in Europe. However, the seats feel like high-quality leather. Center sections of the seats are perforated to support ventilation. The seams and stitching are tight and uniform. There are also design aspects of rounded rectangles in the seats that give them a bit more visual interest compared to some traditional car seats. Straight lines can be a challenge with soft materials, but they seem to be executed very well. Speaking of soft, the seats noticeably combine different densities of padding to provide softness in the seat bottom and support in the bolsters. Even though the seat bottoms look fairly flat, you sink in just enough in the soft center portion to be held in place laterally by the stiffer sides without feeling tight or confined. Lumbar support is also good and adjustable for those of us with bad backs. This lets the seats look clean and simple with straight lines, without sacrificing comfort or support. Clearly, some thought went into designing these seats. In the rear, a 40/20/40 split lets customers place long items down the center console, while still having use to the two outboard rear seats. The L03 seats also offer heating, ventilation, and massage.

But the softness is not limited to the seats. Above the hip line, high quality soft touch materials are abundant. On the doors, different levels of cushioning are offered in different locations. Noticeable padding is on the door tops, which makes resting your arm on the door top with your hand on the A pillar comfortable. You could also rest your arm on the door top with your hand comfortably on the top of the steering wheel. While keeping both hands on the wheel is recommended, whether you prefer to drive with your hands at 10 and 2, 9 and 3, or 5 and 7, all felt surprisingly comfortable on the relatively small, rectangular, 2-spoke wheel.

The European model adds a rectangular 8.8” display that moves with the steering wheel and is easy to read. As someone who prefers not to have to look at a center touchscreen while driving, I thought I would like this. However, it seems largely unnecessary, due to the bright, full color 26.8” W-HUD. While HUDs are hard to capture on camera, it clearly displays both speed and navigation, in addition to rear view images when needed. While the look is still minimalist, XPENG also adds stalk controls for wipers, lights, turn signals and shifting. Buttons on the steering wheel let the driver control the air conditioning and audio system (all the infotainment many of us grew up with), as well as drive modes and VLA 2.0 activation. Voice controls allow users to control basically anything else you could need while driving. Essentially, not only is the driver display unnecessary, but the center touch screen becomes largely unnecessary while driving. You could keep both hands on the wheel and eyes on the road at all times, with the HUD providing the necessary information. The center screen use could be limited just to configuring the system or entertainment while stationary. XPENG could realistically have an OTA update with a mode where only the HUD is displayed while driving with the other screens turned off, which could be especially useful at night (hint XPENG).

Speaking of voice control, XPENG also touted its XOS 6.0 smart cockpit, which enables full “human-like” conversational operation, although this was not enabled yet. Smart Cockpit XOS 6.0 features Google Maps integration. By combining XPENG’s user interface with Google data, the car can provide the desired experience, while reducing data concerns. It will be interesting to see how these work on the road in the final versions.

While there is plenty of technology around the car, there does not seem to be cumbersome technology for the sake of having technology. The semi-hidden door handles are manual, even if the windows seal automatically when closing. The hood release is manual, although the rear hatch is powered. The charge door is manual. The glove box opens manually, without having to go into the touch screen. The one area that lets this theme down is the front air vents, especially as the metal manual rear air vents have a satisfying tactile feel. Sometimes, it is just faster and easier to have mechanical operation.

Similarly, the car is not overly styled for the sake of style. The headlights are functional, without the high mounted light bars and hidden headlights lower down in the front facia. While the stamping around the wheels makes it look a little more interesting, it also stiffens the wheel well. The fender curves make the vehicle look more athletic but also make it easier to see where the wheels are from the driver’s seat. Aerodynamic features are subtle, and the ducts or trim pieces are functional. This helps the vehicle achieve a drag coefficient of 0.228, superior to the Ferrari Luce without ending up looking weird in the process. As XPENGs design head JuanMa López comes from Ferrari, I am sure he is happy with how his design came out in comparison.

The materials and construction combine various soft touch materials above the hip point, only getting into hard plastics as you approach the floor. Soft materials extend from the dashboard all the way to the rear seats. The few hard surfaces above the beltline include a textured strip for the ambient lighting and real metal speaker grilles and knobs. While the door pockets and bottom of the center console are harder plastic, which provides a level of durability, they also contain removable rubber mats that help hold items in place, reduce noise and make cleaning easier. The headliner has been described as velvet or microfiber, but it feels premium regardless of what you want to call it.

The center console contains a wireless phone charging pad and several plugs of different formats for wired low voltage power needs. The European version does not include the refrigerator or scent diffuser found on some Chinese models.

Around the vehicle are multiple mounting points for accessories. There are several standard metal ¼” threaded mounting points throughout the vehicle, which would allow various accessories like action cameras to be installed. A number of magnetic mounting points are positioned subtly around the car for things like tissue boxes and sunglass cases. In the back of the vehicle are metal knobs to hold things like dry cleaning and small plastic hooks farther down the B pillar to hold bags. Seat back pockets are deep. Meanwhile, powered attachment points in the seatbacks allow people to mount accessories like entertainment screens. In the rear cargo area is a place to attack peg boards to mount additional items.

While the car looks sleek, the beltline is not overly high. Out the front, visibility is excellent, and the front fender flares, combined with short overhangs and a low hood, make it easy to see where the corners of the car and the front wheels are. That would make the vehicle easy to place on the road without relying on sensors. The front windows extend behind the driver, providing good visibility. The L03 structure relies heavily on ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS). Not only does this provide 5-star crash ratings across the global standards, but it also lets them make the door openings a little larger and the pillars a little narrower, improving visibility.

While the side rear view mirror housings are relatively small, they are frameless and provide a good view down the sides of the car. In the model I saw, the center rearview mirror was glass and the visibility out the back was somewhat limited. We will have to wait to drive the car to see how well cameras make up for that while reversing. However, all L03 models come with self-parking, which should make reversing a little easier.

Visibility also isn’t lacking for rear passengers. As rear passengers sit up a bit higher than in some EVs, there is good visibility out the front of the vehicle. This is also aided by relatively narrow B pillars. The ability for rear passengers to see the horizon ahead of them helps to reduce motion sickness. The rear side windows and the panoramic silver-coated sunroof also extend behind the eyeline of the rear passengers, creating a feeling of openness.

However there are more interesting details to explore in Part 2 of this article.

data.title: NIO Launches the ES8 Five-Seat Version
data.description:
On July 9, 2026, NIO officially launched the five-seat version of the NIO ES8, its flagship premium SUV. The Executive Premium variant starts at RMB 382,800, or RMB 274,800 with Battery as a Service (BaaS). The Executive Signature variant starts at RMB 422,800, or RMB 314,800 with BaaS. Deliveries of the ES8 Five-Seat Version will begin on July 10.

Users who confirm their orders and purchase the NIO ES8 Five-Seat Version by July 31, 2026 will receive the following benefits: five years of complimentary NOP+ subscription, an interior comfort package valued at RMB 2,680, including a pillow set, plush pile mats, a front trunk protective mat, and a smart fragrance diffuser set. First owners of the ES8 Five-Seat Version will also enjoy six years of complimentary connectivity service, a six-year or 150,000 km vehicle warranty, a 10-year unlimited-mileage warranty for the powertrain system, and lifetime complimentary roadside assistance. NIO is also offering exclusive benefits for existing users purchasing the ES8 Five-Seat Version: 30,000 bonus points for existing NIO ES8 Founders’ Edition users, 20,000 points for existing users of other NIO models, and 10,000 points for existing ONVO and firefly users.

From pioneering the segment to leading it, the ES8 is the world’s first smart electric vehicle to evolve through three generations. The first-generation ES8 was not only the beginning of NIO, but also a pioneer in China’s premium smart electric vehicle market and the premium three-row battery electric SUV segment. Over the past eight years, more than 210,000 users have placed their trust in the ES8.

In 2025, William Li, NIO’s Founder, Chairman, and CEO, predicted that battery electric vehicles would reach a tipping point where the benefits of the technology would outweigh any inconvenience associated with EV charging. The rapid adoption of large three-row battery electric SUVs was the clearest sign of that transition. Just 275 days after its launch, the third-generation ES8 reached 120,000 deliveries. It ranked first in both the large SUV segment and the segment above RMB 400,000 for six consecutive months, and also topped both segments in retail sales in the first half of 2026, marking the arrival of the battery electric era for large three-row SUVs.

The ES8 has accelerated the electrification of the premium large three-row SUV segment. In the premium five-seat SUV market, however, users have long been waiting for a truly groundbreaking battery electric offering. Today, the ES8 once again raises the bar with the launch of the five-seat version, further expanding the possibilities of premium battery electric SUVs. As William Li said, “Just as the ES8 helped usher premium large three-row SUVs into the battery electric era, the ES8 Five-Seat Version will accelerate the transition of premium large five-seat SUVs to battery electric.”

A Five-Seater Designed for Success

The ES8 Five-Seat Version carries forward NIO’s original design language with a confident and distinctive presence. Signature front fascia elements such as the X-Bar and Double-Dash daytime running lights give the vehicle a bold yet refined character. Seven sky-inspired exterior colors, paired with optional obsidian trim, add sophistication through every detail. Inside, the cabin adopts an open yet enveloping design that creates a welcoming sense of comfort for every occupant. The Skyline screen, triple-layer ambient lighting, the metallic speaker covers, and the wraparound second-row seating area come together to create a cabin that is both elegant and unmistakably distinctive.

Five-Seat SUV Space Reimagined

Through breakthrough engineering and technological innovation, the ES8 Five-Seat Version redefines what a premium five-seat SUV can offer in terms of space. It delivers an industry-leading 7.05 m² of usable interior space, the largest ever in its class, along with a second row that offers exceptional roominess well beyond segment standards. Cargo capacity is equally impressive. A 230L power-operated front trunk comfortably accommodates two 20-inch suitcases and one 14-inch suitcase, while the 1,334L rear trunk, the largest in the segment, provides more than twice the carrying capacity of a conventional large SUV. Even with five occupants on board, the ES8 Five-Seat Version can accommodate 17 carry-on suitcases and four 14-inch suitcases, becoming the first vehicle in its segment to deliver “five occupants, 21 suitcases” capability.

A modular cargo divider system, together with a comprehensive range of purpose-designed accessories, enables more than 128 flexible storage configurations. Whether for golf outings, lure fishing, stargazing, or transforming the cargo area into a spacious mobile dressing room, the ES8 Five-Seat Version adapts effortlessly to a wide variety of lifestyles and travel scenarios. Its Haptex tri-fold parcel shelf and Haptex cargo partition, seamlessly integrated with the second-row cabin, create a level of passenger-and-cargo separation more commonly associated with premium sedans.

Premium Comfort, Elevated Experience

The ES8 Five-Seat Version sets a new benchmark for comfort in the premium five-seat SUV segment. Both front seats feature a zero-gravity mode with 20-way power adjustment and power leg rests, allowing occupants to recline into a relaxed seating position with one tap. In the second row, reclining seats offer a backrest angle of up to 138 degrees, complemented by power leg supports and footrests that provide full-body support for truly relaxing journeys. A smart four-zone climate control system and an electric drawer fridge create a comfortable cabin environment throughout the year, while the air quality system and zero-formaldehyde cabin help ensure cleaner, healthier air for everyone on board. The ES8 Five-Seat Version also features 1.7 m² of electrochromic glass with three tint levels, together with an active road noise cancellation system capable of reducing peak road noise by up to 8 dB, delivering outstanding levels of privacy, serenity, and overall cabin refinement.

Cinematic Experience, Total Immersion

The ES8 Five-Seat Version is equipped with the NIO LYRA 8.1.4.2 Sound System, featuring 27 premium speakers and 2,600 watts of rated output at 10% THD, delivering flagship-level acoustic performance. Powered by Dirac Dimensions, the system transforms conventional stereo sources into high-fidelity immersive audio, while Dirac RRM faithfully recreates the acoustic character of legendary recording studios, world-class live performances, and renowned concert halls. Complementing the audio experience are an industry-leading 15.6-inch dual-layer OLED center display and a segment-leading 21.4-inch automotive-grade mini-LED overhead screen. Together with triple Dolby-certified technologies, they create an immersive audio and visual experience, elevating in-vehicle entertainment to a new level.

Exceptional Performance, Confident Control

Born with NIO’s performance DNA, the ES8 Five-Seat Version delivers outstanding driving dynamics brought by class-leading lightweight engineering. At an average of 260 kg lighter than comparable vehicles in its segment, it lays the foundation for both exceptional handling and energy efficiency. Built on NIO’s advanced full-domain 900V high-voltage architecture, the five-seater combines dual electric drive systems with a 102-kWh long-range battery as standard, delivering exhilarating performance alongside a CLTC range of up to 655 km. Built on NIO’s PrimeRide Chassis System, the ES8 Five-Seat Version combines flagship chassis hardware with advanced sensing and control systems, including NIO’s in-house developed Vehicle Motion Control (VMC), to elevate both handling and ride comfort. It features Smart U-Turn, motion sickness relief, lane-level 4D Smart Suspension, and Wet Mode. The newly introduced Multi-Terrain Mode further expands its capabilities across wet roads, sand, water crossings, mud, gravel, and snow, delivering a smoother, more comfortable, and more reassuring driving experience.

Flagship Intelligence, A Generation Ahead

The ES8 Five-Seat Version is powered by NT.Cedar, NIO’s flagship smart system, and features the world’s first 5 nm automotive-grade smart driving chip, NX9031. Together with NIO’s next-generation AI-native digital architecture and NIO OS, its full-domain operating system, the vehicle delivers refreshing intelligence on all fronts. Powered by NOMI Intelligence, NOMI continues to evolve into an even more capable AI companion. At the same time, the ES8 Five-Seat Version is built with end-to-end security, ensuring a smarter experience that is more secure, reliable, and trustworthy.

Comprehensive Safety, All-Around Protection

Through innovations in the smart system and vehicle architecture, the ES8 Five-Seat Version seamlessly integrates active and passive safety. The ES8 has earned top ratings across all five categories in C-IASI testing, including multiple G+ ratings, and successfully completed a triple-direction consecutive collision challenge at 100 km/h. A robust occupant safety cage featuring a six-longitudinal, 10-transverse, and eight-vertical structure, together with nine airbags, provides comprehensive occupant protection. Additional innovations, including automatic pull-over following a tire blowout at high speed and an in-car Guardian Mode powered by foundation models, further enhance safety in complex driving scenarios. The High-Definition Projection Lights (HDPL) boast an ultra-wide light carpet spanning up to six lanes, delivering industry-leading enhanced illumination. Working closely with the smart driving system, the S-shaped light carpet visually communicates the vehicle’s intended lane changes and trajectory, enhancing safety for everyone on the road.

Flagship Smart Driving, Reliable and Efficient

The ES8 Five-Seat Version boasts NIO’s flagship smart driving hardware and software architecture. The Cedar AQUILA Super Sensing is equipped with 31 high-performance sensors to provide comprehensive 360-degree, all-weather perception. Powered by the industry-leading NIO WorldModel (NWM) and a training framework combining closed-loop reinforcement learning with supervised fine-tuning, the system continuously evolves for increasingly capable smart driving experiences. Point-to-Point Navigate on Pilot Plus (NOP+) delivers exceptionally smooth vehicle control, precise lane selection, and intelligent interaction with surrounding traffic. Intelligent Parking Assist allows users to choose left- or right-offset parking according to the parking space. Intent-Aware Wayfinding, which can be activated from virtually anywhere in a parking lot, automatically guides a vehicle to pass gates, climb ramps, and exit the facility, and seamlessly transitions to campus roaming and urban navigation.

The NIO ES8 Five-Seat Version offers a compelling upgrade for owners of premium gasoline SUVs and luxury sedans alike, delivering more refined, efficient, and human-centric mobility across every journey. With the ES9, ES8, and ES8 Five-Seat Version, NIO has established a comprehensive premium SUV lineup spanning premium five-seater SUVs, premium three-row SUVs, and executive flagship SUVs. Backed by industry-leading technological innovation and a forward-looking product portfolio, NIO continues to usher the large SUV segment into the battery electric era.
